trajectory_summary: The call runs clean through verify and hits a hard DQ on credit (530 stated — 140 points below threshold, well outside bureau-variance ambiguity). The DQ itself is defensible. What leaks energy is the exit: Colin receives a genuine vulnerability disclosure about unemployment and financial recovery, offers no empathy, skips the wife's score entirely, and closes with a generic 'come back when you're above 670' — no locked callback, no human acknowledgment. Andrew exits politely but without any reason to return.
outcome_dispute: The DQ decision is defensible — 530 stated is 140 points below the 670 threshold, far outside the bureau-variance zone the schema's spencer_operator_note flags as ambiguous (which applies to near-threshold cases, not to a 140-point gap). The correct dispute is not 'premature_DQ' but 'clean_DQ_poor_exit': the disqualification was procedurally sound, but the exit quality was poor. Colin did not check the wife's credit score (she is on the title and was confirmed at 01:24), did not lock a callback with a specific timeframe, and did not acknowledge Andrew's vulnerability disclosure before closing. A clean DQ with a locked future touchpoint and a human moment would have preserved the lead for a future cycle.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: DQ exit quality — wife's score and locked callback
primary_coaching_focus.why: Colin DQ's on Andrew's stated score without checking the wife's independently — she is confirmed on title at 01:24 and was included in the qualifier question at 02:22. One follow-up question ('do you know roughly where your wife's score is?') may have preserved the lead. Beyond that, the exit has no locked callback, no timeframe, and no acknowledgment of Andrew's recovery trajectory. The DQ decision is defensible; the exit execution is not. This is the highest-leverage coaching moment on this call because it's a concrete, repeatable procedural fix — not a style note.

key_moments.quote: That's a that's a good issue. We recently were unemployed, and so, I cannot drop our credit score. We just recently got back working again. So
key_moments.stewart_read: Andrew volunteers a significant personal hardship — unemployment, credit damage, recent recovery. This is a trust gift per the schema. Colin's response is to ask for the score number ('do you know what it is sitting around?') and proceed to exit. No acknowledgment of the difficulty, no bridge to how solar could help once they're back on their feet, no human moment. The reactive read holds: Colin treated the disclosure as a data point rather than a relationship moment.

Colin confirms the wife is on title at 01:24 and never returns to her. When the credit DQ arrives at 03:31, he DQ's on Andrew's score alone without asking about the wife's score. The wife is a co-title holder — her credit score is independently relevant to qualification. This is a procedural miss, not a strategic deferral. The reactive read holds.

Andrew states his bill is 'about a 120' at 01:49. Colin acknowledges '120' and then immediately says '223' — a figure that appears nowhere else in the conversation and is never explained or attributed. This is either a data artifact (a prior bill figure on file, a system field Colin is reading, or a transcription error), or Colin is reading from a record that doesn't match what Andrew just said. The schema's numeric_discipline directive is explicit: numbers must be grounded. An unexplained figure introduced mid-bill-capture is a coaching and data-quality flag.

key_moments.quote: Okay. Well, yeah. I would just recommend looking into solar once that is above $6.70. Just that way, you guys can get all the incentives.
key_moments.stewart_read: The exit line is the clearest signal of reactive intent in the call. Colin has a confirmed interested homeowner, a disclosed reason for the credit gap (unemployment, now resolved), a wife on title whose score is unknown, and a bill anchor that was captured but never flipped. He exits with a generic 'come back later' that gives Andrew no reason to return, no timeframe, and no human connection to the call. No locked callback. No acknowledgment of the recovery trajectory Andrew described.
